text: TEMPERATURES FROM 700 TO $560^{\circ}C$
EVALUATION OF HASTELLOY N ALLOYS AFTER NINE YEARS EXPOSURE TO BOTH A MOLTEN FLUORIDE SALT AND AIR AT TEMPERATURES FROM 700 TO $560^{\circ}C$
Comparison of Mass Transfer in Loop 1255 and a Type 304L Stainless Steel Loop 31

section: EVALUATION OF HASTELLOY N ALLOYS AFTER NINE YEARS EXPOSURE TO BOTH A MOLTEN FLUORIDE SALT AND AIR AT TEMPERATURES FROM 700 TO $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
text: EVALUATION OF HASTELLOY N ALLOYS AFTER NINE YEARS EXPOSURE TO BOTH A MOLTEN FLUORIDE SALT AND AIR AT TEMPERATURES FROM 700 TO $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$

A Hastelloy N thermal convection loop, some portions of which consisted of a Hastelloy N alloy modified with $2\%$ Nb for improved weld properties and containing LiF-23 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2 - 5$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4 - 1}$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4 - 1}$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ , was operated for 9.2 years at a maximum temperature of $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a minimum of $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The failure of the loop was attributed to a reaction between impurities in a ceramic bushing and the modified Hastelloy $\mathbf{N} - 2\%$ Nb tubing.
The attack, which occurred in the hot section, was manifested in the formation of voids, in a zone of maximum depth of 4 mils.
No difference in corrosion could be seen in the standard Hastelloy N and the modified Hastelloy $\mathbf{N} - 2\%$ Nb alloy.
A two-layer oxide of 2 mils thickness was the maximum formed under the heaters in 9.2 years exposure to air.

Hastelloy N (initially known as INOR-8, nominal composition $72\%$ Ni- $16\%$ Mo- $7\%$ Cr- $5\%$ Fe) was developed at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) in the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion (ANP) Program and was viewed as the most promising container material for molten fluorides exposed to the severe $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ ANP conditions.

However, during routine qualification of welders on Hastelloy N material in 1961 (before widespread use of the alloy) the presence of a possible weld-cracking problem was detected in one heat of the alloy.

Because of the possible widespread use of the modified alloy in a molten fluoride salt environment, corrosion tests were scheduled to evaluate the resistance to salt attack of Hastelloy N that had been modified by the addition of $2\%$ Nb.
In addition to corrosion data on Hastelloy N- $2\%$ Nb, it was planned that these tests would also supply data on the corrosion properties of various types of weld junctions listed below:
The test system used was a Hastelloy N thermal convection loop (designated loop 1255) similar to the ones shown in Fig.
The maximum temperature chosen for this test was $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the minimum $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The velocity of the salt resulting from this temperature difference was approximately 5 bpm.
The experiment was started April 11, 1962, and, with only a few minor interruptions, operated until July 20, 1971.

The results of this experiment are still quite significant in that recent developments<sup>3</sup> have indicated that certain Hastelloy N alloys containing additions of Ti, Hf, Nb, and Zr have good mechanical properties (better than standard Hastelloy N) after being irradiated at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figure 2 is a photograph of a typical thermal convection loop with heaters and thermocouples installed but without insulation.
The major portion of the loop was constructed of $\frac{3}{8}$ -in. sched 10 standard Hastelloy N pipe (approx 0.675 in.
Y-8460 (Superior Tube) and an experimental heat specified as Haynes SP-19.
The top 9 in. of the hot leg contained two tube inserts.
The top insert was Hastelloy N-2% Nb, and the bottom one was standard Hastelloy N.
The inserts were 3-in.-long cylinders, 0.595 in.
OD, 0.025 in. wall, which were fitted into machined portions of the loop tubing, thus retaining the same flow cross section throughout the loop.
The next 6-in. section of the hot leg was Hastelloy N-2% Nb tubing, and the remainder of the loop was standard Hastelloy N.
The Hastelloy N-2% Nb alloy was from experimental heat MP-13.
The compositions of the alloys are given in Table 1.
In forming the modified Hastelloy N tubing, a $\frac{1}{4}$ -in. slab was first rolled and cross-rolled until the final thickness was approximately 0.040 in.
The sheet was then formed into a tube and welded with Hastelloy N-2% Nb weld wire.

The hot portion of each loop was heated by sets of clamshell heaters with the input power controlled by silicon controlled rectifiers (SCR units) and the temperature controlled by a Leeds and Northrup Speedomax H series 60 type C.A.T.
Prior to filling, the loop was heated under a vacuum of $10\mu$ for leak-checking and bake-out.
The loop was then filled with flush salt, which was dumped after $2\mathrm{hr}$ , and then was filled with the operating salt.
The salt used was the proposed MSRE fuel salt with a nominal composition of LiF-23 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ -5 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ -1 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ -1 mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ .
To remove the salt, various portions of the loop were cut into small pieces, placed in a graphite-lined nickel container, and heated under argon for 4 hr at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Corrosive attack on exterior of Hastelloy N tubing underneath ceramic bushings after 552 hr at 675 to $785^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
In 1964, ORNL conducted a corrosion program in support of the SNAP-8 electrical-generating system.
Several instances of excessive corrosion were noted on the exterior of the "chromized" Hastelloy N tubing underneath ceramic bushings similar to those used in loop 1255.
The first instance noted was on a loop after 552 hr of operation at design temperature, $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ maximum, $593^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ minimum.

Exterior pitting was found on the chromized $\frac{3}{4}$ -in.-OD, 0.072-in.-wall Hastelloy N tubing under the ceramic bushings (grade A Lava, unfired) used to space the electric heaters from the tubing (Fig.

The attack was noticeable on the tube wall at the location where the temperature was estimated to have reached $675^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the attack increased toward the high-temperature end of the tubing, where the temperature was calculated to have been approximately $785^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
5) was 0.037 in. deep, as determined by external measurements and x rays of the area.
Loop operated $1300\mathrm{hr}$ at $785^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Subsequent to this, all ceramic bushings of the grade A Lava type were fired at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ prior to installation.
The exterior corrosion occurred on the $3/4$ -in.-OD, 0.072-in.-thick wall of the hot-spot section, which was operating at $785^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A NaK leak developed after $1300\mathrm{hr}$ of operation.
Figure 6 shows both the hole through which the leak occurred and an adjacent area with accelerated oxidation that had not quite penetrated the wall.

We also note that the areas modified by heating (outer surface) and by corrosion (inner surface) have approximately the same affected thickness (5 mils) for both alloys.
On the outer surface, the depth of disturbance (as seen by the disappearance of the precipitate, probably carbides) is 5 mils.
On the inner surface, the layer that contains the voids is 2 or 3 mils deep, with carbide removal (probably redistribution) extending another 2 mils.

In Ni-27% Cr alloys exposed to oxygen at $800 - 1200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , NiO forms at the outer surface, followed by $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ adjacent to the matrix.
In oxidized Ni-Cr alloys with 3-10% chromium, the outer layer is NiO, followed by $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ particles in a nickel matrix next to a surface.
The etching of the oxidized samples disclosed extensive intergranular penetration which extended about 3 mils into the matrix.
No oxide penetration greater than 3 mils was seen.

The formation of subsurface voids as seen in loop 1255 is initiated by the oxidation of chromium along exposed surfaces through oxidation-reduction reactions with impurities or constituents of the molten fluoride mixture.

Voids of this same type have been developed in Inconel 600 by high-temperature oxidation tests and high-temperature vacuum tests in which chromium is selectively removed.<sup>9</sup> Voids similar to these have also been developed in copper-brass diffusion couples and by thedezincification of brass.<sup>10</sup> All of these phenomena arise from the so-called Kirkendall effect, whereby solute atoms of a given type diffuse out at a faster rate than other atoms comprising the crystal lattice can diffuse in to fill vacancies which result from the outward diffusion.
36 for the salt mixture NaF-46 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ -4 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , can be explained in terms of the corrosion reactions discussed above.

The initial rapid attack shown for both types of loops stems from the reaction of chromium with impurities in the melt (reactions 1 and 2) and with the UF $_4$ constituent of the salt (reaction 3) to establish a quasi-equilibrium amount of CrF $_2$ in the salt.
This can be seen by referring to Table 5, where chromium concentrations are shown for fluoride salts at a series of operating times.
Note that between the 50 hr and 1000 hr operating times, no significant increase in CrF $_2$ content has occurred.

section: Comparison of Mass Transfer in Loop 1255 and a Type 304L Stainless Steel Loop
text: A thermal convection loop of type 304L stainless steel has contained salt from the same batch as loop 1255 for over eight years at a maximum temperature of $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a minimum temperature of $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: Comparison of Mass Transfer in Loop 1255 and a Type 304L Stainless Steel Loop
text: Comparisons between loop 1255 and the type 304L stainless steel loop show that the void formation in the stainless steel after 5700 hr equaled that of the Hastelloy N after 9.2 years.
The schematic temperature profile of loop 1255 as a function of loop position is shown in Fig.

Weight loss of type 304L stainless steel specimens as a function of operation time at various temperatures in LiF-23 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2 - 5$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_4 - 1$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF}_4 - 1$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF}_4$ salt.
Microstructure of type 304L stainless steel specimen in loop 1258, exposed to fuel salt for 5700 hr at $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A simple calculation based on the increase of $1700~\mathrm{ppm}$ chromium in the salt during operation gives us some insight on the amount of material removed.
The amount of material that would have to be removed to equal the number of grams of chromium found in the salt was 2.2 mils of Hastelloy N over one-half the length of the loop.
Thus, the average depth of complete chromium depletion in the hot leg would be 2.2 mils.
40, this means we could expect a maximum depletion of 4.4 mils at the hottest position at the top of the hot leg, $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , decreasing to zero at $630^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
We have assumed that all positions at temperatures below $630^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ gain weight.
8, the shim stock (foil) that covered the thermocouple can still be seen.
The failure in loop 1255 looked very much like the loop failure referenced in an earlier portion of this report.
Thus, on the basis of the earlier work and our findings, we attribute the failure to a reaction between the bushing and the modified Hastelloy N-2% Nb tubing.
It is worth while to mention that $\mathsf{MoO}_3$ melts at $795^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ and the eutectic between $\mathsf{MoO}_2$ and $\mathsf{MoO}_3$ is at $778^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ .
Our observations of the air oxidation of the Hastelloy N during the 9.2 years of operation disclosed about a 2-mil-thick layer of mixed oxide as the worst condition.
23-28), the oxide layer thickness decreased, along with a decrease in intergranular penetration and chromium depletion.

As part of the molten salt corrosion program, the outside of Hastelloy N tubing containing fluoride salt is routinely examined to determine its behavior under air oxidation conditions.11 Table 7 gives some typical results.
The only evidence of intergranular penetration was seen in the material exposed for 11,300 hr, and in this case the penetration was quite small.
Thus, we conclude that Hastelloy N in service at temperatures below $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has shown good resistance to air oxidation, with penetration not exceeding 2 mils/year.

We saw, from microscopic examination of the loop tubing, that mass transfer of material (material removal and material deposition) did occur during the 9.2-year exposure of the Hastelloy N alloys to the LiF-23 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ -5 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ -1 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ -1 mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ salt.
A two-layer oxide of 2 mils thickness was the maximum formed in 9.2 years exposure to air.
The failure of the loop was tentatively attributed to a reaction between the impurities in a ceramic bushing and the modified Hastelloy N-2% Nb tubing.
